item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I am missing something- I'm not sure why the expedition members perceive themselves to be dead at the end? But this was suspenseful enough to keep me reading even though i usually lose interest in the longer articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A wild ride all the way through!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I’m like 5 years late, but I am very happy you somehow got bobble into this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not merely a mere thought provoker, this SCP article straight up delivers a thrilling, self-contained novella. Incredible work of art.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are too many skips that are just locked up and left alone. Actually neutralizing a serious threat for once, described within the SCP article itself, is pretty damn refreshing. Kaktus did a good job, in my estimation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This still holds up, and it's still one of my all time favorite SCPs

+1
I find myself wondering how on earth did you make me engaged throughout such a long story. Amazing work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a really clever idea and not something I had intended when I wrote it. Really neat idea!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure if anyone else has hypothesized this, but is Leech Boy a variant of SCP-1003? Could be a coincidence, of course, and I'm not sure if the author has said anything about his origin, but I quite like this idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scp is 100 times better if you read all of Ross's lines with Buck's voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers for the skip
What happened to Site-13? Now that's the question.

I really like this article. I'm personally a bigger fan of long skips as opposed to short ones, cause I like to see the story unfurl - whether through containment procedures, interview logs, exploration logs, or downloaded files, all of which appear.

What happened to Site-13? Now that's the question. I like the idea of some darker Foundation, a Secure, Destroy, Toss-In-The-Body-Pit Foundation. Emerson is unlikable, and I liked the more interesting choices (Bright's pendant is traditional immortality instead of soul pendant, as an example.)

I also really find that kinetoglyph skip interesting. I'd read an article just about him, it feels nifty.

My overall thoughts, it doesn't matter what happened, because reading about what happened makes all those MTF losses worth it :)

also, is it intentional that this is SCP-1730?

Ooh, and also:

Unidentified Figure: Hee hee. Hee hah. You're welcome. Heehee. Oh, oh, (as if in pain) Tanny, oh Tanny, it's so long down here with us. It's so long and sharp, Tanny. Why did you make me hurt, hee hee. Oh- (voice fades as the screen is covered in static)

So this is new. Could it be related by any chance to what happened to Site-19?

item.title: I’m sure it’s been said but
item.content:encoded: Did the toy robot(not sure about it’s number) remind anyone else of a one punch man villain?? Just me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, dismiss me however you wish

If you insist.

In truth, the updates were made to reflect my original intentions for this article. The only reason why it wasn't like this from the beginning is that I wasn't sure how I wanted to end it, so I broke it into two halves. Thankfully, the response has been nearly unanimously positive and I could not be happier with how it turned out.

Thanks for reading it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What- what the fuck?
What the hell happened to 1730? I re-read it because it's one of my favorites, and now there's a bunch of clunky additions?

I'm genuinely shocked that the additions were made by the same author, because it certainly doesn't feel like it was written by the same person at all. As I read through this, my soul was propelled straight out of my body as MTF-1337 'Mary Sues' came in and handled the SCP. Seriously, the original article was a tight, very neat cautionary tale/bit of pants-shitting horror, ending up with the whole place taped off because it's too fucked to save. And now it's??? Just neutralized??? I genuinely could not bring myself to read the last few collapsibles.

Well, dismiss me however you wish, this skip's too big to fail. For me, though, a hard -1.

item.title: Y-24
item.content:encoded:
According to Houston, one of the Y-24 guys survived, but we never learn who it is or what happened to them. Feels like a weird loose thread to leave hanging there. :/

[Obviously, I love the story and only realized this after reading it like the 90th time :P]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's certainly one way to put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So really, things were fine until the government got involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, wow. I really liked that leviathan deathmatch although it felt hard to follow at times. I'm currently feeling a very high discomfort not knowing where Site-13 is or what in the hell happened to it. Hell let lose.

For all we know it may be floating in space. I don't even know what to say, the whole thing is kinda fucked up.

Great article and def one of the best on this site, certainly in my top 10. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Five years later, this is still an excellent article for exploration. It is hard to say what changes have taken place in recent years and what changes have taken place in style. However, this is still my favorite article.
But I have a small question. How did Site-13 transfer the 001 gatekeeper? I am very curious about this, but it does not affect the reading of the full text in any way.
I like the scenes of the fighting between the three gods very much, and the descriptions are very detailed. The long documents make the atmosphere even more horrible.
You are the author I admire most. I hope you can also create excellent works like arrogant people and look forward to your new works.

item.title: What Happened to Site-13?
item.content:encoded:
I think that we can answer this firmly, after 5 years.

Missioncrab stated it best:

Shit hit the fan, that's what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. Definitely so long that I took more than a day to work through it all, but it's worth it. The concept is not entirely original, the ending doesn't feel all surprising as what I would expect. But what seals the deal here is the journey you've led us into. Thrills and chills all the way throughout, somewhat emotional and intimidating as hell. Incredible world building.

Easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really sorry, but I can't accept the first sequence of exploration attempts as something any rational in-universe person would sign off on. They have or can requisition drones, but they send a human exploration team in first. We know that there was no preliminary unmanned sweep of anything because it's the MTF who find out the front door is unlocked. Given that this is apparently a Foundation site that is non-functional, shouldn't exist, and shouldn't be here, I feel like that's a questionable HR decision, but it's in line with other exploration articles that open up with manned missions. I can go with this.

MTFs are supposed to be highly-competent, well-trained anomaly investigators, but the first team to go in acts really stupidly. First of all, they get into an elevator. They do not try to find a flight of stairs; they just hop into the first elevator they see in a neglected building of unknown safety. The power station hasn't even been examined yet, so they also don't know if they can expect the elevator to stay powered. Sure, the writing on the wall warns them to "get below," but there's a reason you're not supposed to rely on elevators in an emergency. If they're seriously concerned that it's not safe to spend long enough on the ground floor to locate some stairs, then they should A) say that, and B) withdraw and send a drone in to scout out a route to some stairs, but they don't do either of those. Then they skip the first two basements for no apparent reason. Sure, the captain suggests that they see how far they can get, but even if it was stated that B3 was the deepest floor they could reach, they're still setting themselves up to die. The problem with a bottom-up approach is that they A) might have to go up by a different escape route if something happens, B) don't know what those routes are, and C) don't know if those routes are clear or safe. And then they split up, which is approximately where I realized they were all wearing red shirts. Then everything goes to hell and they all die. This sequence drops the bread crumbs it set out to drop, but it doesn't do so with the narrative gravity I feel a total MTF wipe deserves. It just makes MTF D12 look to me like a bunch of genre-blind buffoons.

Only after losing an entire exploration team does anyone think to check the power station, and instead of sending in the microdrones or regular drones by themselves while their operators remain outside, they still send three more humans into a building that might explode and cave in on top of them, and that's before you figure in the odds of it being as much of an anomalous death trap as the main building proved to be. I cannot think of a single in-universe justification for sending a manned mission into there instead of either using the drones they had or sitting tight and waiting for better drones to arrive. Not one. But they go in, lose contact, and are assumed KIA. They also look at wall grafitti, even though the previously-recieved warning to not look at the walls should have put everyone on-guard for potential cognitohazards.

Then, finally, an unmanned drone is set in. Again, despite the now-repeated warnings to not look at the walls, a bunch of people look at the raw footage/photography of some funny symbols an anomaly drew on the wall. Only after those people have blown up and died does anyone in-universe think to consult cognitohazard specialists or apply cognitohazard-screening algorithms. At this point, I have lost any sense of immersion or suspense. I can't suspend disbelief for these characters anymore. Honestly this is about the point where I want to stop reading and go back to listening to a guy on YouTube explain the Spark Notes version.

Continuing to read anyway, the Mole Rats are the next sacrifices to the triple-verified death trap that was Site-13. I hate harping on this, but this implies a character looked at this place with an apparent 100% mortality rate for actually going inside and a non-zero mortality rate for observing drone-captured images, and then decided "let's stop using drones and send more humans in", and absolutely no-one fired them for it. Also we have Z9-Cap getting SiteComm to repeat exposition for the audience that should have been in a thorough briefing well before this. You do not suit up to go into an anomalous building and then ask what to expect in it or what its original purpose was. I feel like this kind of information is useful to reiterate in such a long article, but there has to be a better way to do it. I guess "there has to be a better way to do it" sums up how I feel about all of the logs up to this point.

Z9-4 falling through the floor and the team not having a way to get him back up works, but the fact it's the Mole Rats makes me feel like it's kinda iffy. Spatial weirdness is their thing, and equipment for emergency vertical navigation feels like it should be in their toolkit. Collapsible ladders, manual winches, stuff like that. They're also supposed to have filters that block out all messages on the walls, just to be safe, but it sounds like Z9-1 is able to read "What happened to Site-13" messages. Maybe I just didn't read that part right, though.

I actually really like the AP-3 log, for the most part. Bobble's last comment is completely uncalled for, but I can't say it's not in-character for him. This is an interesting and engaging log with characters who seem like they more-or-less know what they're doing. The casualties are unique and they actually fight back against something successfully. I had fun reading this part.

T-5's trip down is very much a romp, but that works. Good release of tension before the escape sequence. I love 86243AR-001.

The escape sequence feels good. I don't really know how else to describe it. The action's good, the tension's good, it's fun to read, and covering the "WHAT" with "EMERSON" hit me like a ton of bricks. I love it. That might just be the strongest line in the whole article so far. Leech Monster vs. Fire Angel vs. DEER is beautiful.

On the debriefs, I see what you did there with Josie's butt. Clever.

Overall, the second half is much stronger than the first half. I can see why this article is so popular. I feel much more conflicted about this article than I did… 21 and a half hours ago, if I'm doing my math right. I'm glad I finished reading it. I'm changing my downvote to a neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is old but I feel like it's still worth commenting, because it could help, maybe.

This was genuinely scary in places, and strung me along for a really long time, but the shit with Bobble the Clown was so immersion-breaking I actually found myself rolling my eyes at it, particularly 'rape him to death for me', which seemed distasteful and unnecessary. Before that I was absolutely riveted through the whole thing.

Honestly though I've never had a couple of paragraphs so completely ruin something for me. I'd suggest cutting that addendum down.

Edit after continuing to read: The subsequent addendums (to Bobble) also need a tonal shift. They're well-written, but as other crits have noted it reads like an action movie script instead of Foundation material.

The slow pacing at the beginning is brilliant, and allowing the reader to theorize about what caused the cognitohazards and what exactly Emerson did to the people in Site-13 is a smart move that is then retroactively soured when the Samsara crew arrives and everything starts to move super fast. I really wish I could have just left myself with that creeping horror of Not Knowing.

That said, I was thoroughly engaged again during the end-of-article interviews. I'm not sure how I feel but it's generally positive so I'm going to upvote anyway.

(I'm sorry if this sounds harsh… I haven't posted many proper critiques and I'm not sure what the expected balance/format is here yet. I'll def be reading your other pieces, though, and this one deserves its high rating.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: kaktus, you don't know me. But your articles have been some my favorites on the site, outside of a few by other authors. But this article was the one that got me to actually start using and reading this site regularly. Thank you (but also screw you I'm supposed to be studying for my chem final rn).

item.title: Good read
item.content:encoded:
Honestly a very good, read, especially now that I managed to sit down and read it properly. Takes a lot of cross-workings though (Samsara was confusing my first time around, makes more sense now, along with all the references to other popular SCPs)

I also like the implication that the "Leech Boy" somehow bonded with Vera Hadley which was a both horrifying and extremely intriguing inference. I'd like to see a tale about that if I'm being honest.

item.title: Nitpick
item.content:encoded: The caption text "[click image to enlarge]" doesn't appear to be needed because graph.jpg was originally displayed at a size that is 500 x 446.

item.title: To the author/updater
item.content:encoded:
Hello~
I'm basically a lazy ass with way too much free time currently and found myself endlessly reading this sites stories again.
I believe recently this was updated? I approve of those article updates/additions unless I remember reading this in the past wrong.

Certain things could still use a bit of fine tuning, I won't mention them yet since i'm not finished reading the entire article to see what in all has been added to it story wise. My major pet peeve currently is only (Surprised? Me too!) the Emerson bit. I hate how the burn on his chest is still equated with a burn from fabric. It just doesn't "read right" to me. I get what it is wanted to imply but I think it could be worded differently or left out altogether. I think the "Emerson" brand on his chest being said is sufficient enough. Personally I'd like to read an alternate world amendment or something telling the story of the horrors he faced as he tried to escape his fate.
I highly approve of these expansions in details. I wish for more to be added honestly.
I would buy this novel and thoroughly enjoy it……You know if it was one….For sale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
T-5 Onru: Do not attempt to look at them. You do not
recognize them. Move quickly, come on.

Awwwww yiss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, djkaktus!

The other day, I modified the Info Bar in your article to work correctly. I wanted to contribute to your article, but the revised results don't look very clean. ("Translations:" block)

Your other articles used CSS to hide translation_block. However, there are some articles that do not correspond. These include:
SCP-1730
SCP-4444

Incidentally, some articles may not display the comment in Info Bar correctly. For example, "{$comments}". These include:
Fear Alone
SCP-2399
SCP-7143-J [SFW?]
SCP-2678
Atzak I

The solution to this problem is to write:

comments=@@ @@

I hope you will find it useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Wait a second. Is… Is that the same Paul Manafort as in our timeline? What?

item.title: Re: sentimentality
item.content:encoded:
I'm usually pretty good about saying a lot, but here I am at a loss. Figures.

You're welcome, and thank you. I wouldn't be doing it if weren't for you and everyone like you.

item.title: sentimentality
item.content:encoded:
This was the one.

It's been nearly a year since I read this last, barring the occasional excursion into the logs to revisit memorable quotes. This recent revisitation could not have been more dissimilar to my first read. A year ago, my relationship with the site was entirely through Lord Bung's Confinement, and I was directed here to learn more about the shadow man with the strange hat that I saw in his music video. I was hopelessly lost, but nonetheless entranced by the enormity of what I was experiencing. I didn't know Samsara were cyborgs (although I inferred fairly quickly); I hardly knew who the GOC were; I certainly did not recognize the bodies in the water. Who are these Doctors Bright, Light, and Cimmerian? The hell's an Ethics Committee?

SCP-1730 was the article which directly led to my joining the site with the intention of writing regularly; I wanted desperately to capture the frenzied excitement I felt as the possibilities of the Foundation were laid open to me. Reading this article from the other side of the Veil, now, I can't help but recognize the impact it had on my writing. The inspiration from the logs is obvious in The Beast Beneath The Library, the design of SCP-4260 is obviously inspired by Malidramagiuan, and my own attempts at a canon can only be seen as an imitation of your own shared universe.

Jesus Christ, thank you, Kaktus. One year later and I have an author page and the beginnings of a proper, hard-hitting canon under my proverbial belt. You and your articles — especially this one — are the reason I'm writing today. I cannot express the extent of my appreciation for your contributions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hope doesn't spread to too many other articles

Got some real bad news for you there, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i liked it better before the change in header, which i do not like at all and hope doesn't spread to too many other articles (it breaks the page for me and makes things more difficult to read), but not enough to make me change my upvote. cause this is still one of the best articles on the site.

also, someone should quote one of the comments on the first page with a spoilered list of SCPs. it's pretty deep in page 7, and as more comments get added, people might not wanna try reading them all to find that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy Hell, that one was epic (in the proper sense of the word)!
They say the Foundation is cold, not cruel. It appears this Foundation lost sight of that.
I did like that some MTF guys actually got to come back, most skips go through those poor sods like tissues. Granted it took the superhuman MTF to pull it off but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the "Euclid Containment Status" is also listed as "Critical" on the same list (with the same colour) it seems that it's a general damage report, not a description of the reactor's reaction in scientific terms. In context "Critical" means "currently still working but with serious problems and likely to fail soon."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this scp, including most of the supplemental material. I only got bored during the long power rangers section. This may or may not be why I couldn’t tell the various black entities apart in a scene that was extra full of them.

I came to this scp from a reddit non-Euclidean geometry recommendation thread, so through no fault of the story’s I felt that the weird geometry of the place was underplayed. Just trapping the characters for plot purposes (and all the other escaped SCPs) obscured the eeriness of the setting itself.

Still a +1, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hint: Whatever that liquid is, someone dropped half a cat in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredibly minor nitpick for a 28,000-word article, but if the reactor's critical then it's basically functioning as should be, no? Is the orange because a self-sustaining nuclear reaction isn't something to leave unattended in the conditions at Site-13, or some minor oversight? In my eyes critical would be green unless green is reserved for shutdown.

Critical: reaction is self-sustaining (baseline function level for nuclear reactors)
Supercritical: reaction is increasing in intensity by itself.
Prompt Critical: reaction is going to Chernobyl you if you don't fix it fast.

This is of course in contrast to normal criticality, where literally any of the other systems or functions being critical is a very bad thing no matter what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favorite SCPs on this site, even though it is considered "neturalized". This page has just about anything I could want.

Exploration logs? Check. Run-down building? Check. Bunch of things that should be dead but they aren't? Check. A bunch of SCPs beating the crap out of each other? Check. Dr. Jack Bright? Check. Extremely random occurrences (looking at you, Toy Robot)? Check. Rescue mission? Check. Fucking pipes? Check. Robots? Check. Some guy suffering an anomaly but is otherwise perfectly fine (Houston)? Check.

There's so much to this that I really enjoy, and while it reads off more like a tale than a "traditional" SCP, it works well and I love it.

Only complaint is that it can get confusing here and there if you don't read carefully. I'd give +3 if I could, but +1 will have to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm, this reminds me of why I like exploration logs the best. You're following some guys who know that crap is about to get real and/or not real in a hurry. The fear is contagious, and I really like… I don't know how to explain it other than to say it helps me sort of stay grounded in the story.

Also, unexpected SCP kaiju fight ftw. The only complaint I have is that there was a link to an entire SCP in the middle of this one that we apparently needed to read to see why the heck the Foundation was once again sending dudes to die at the bottom of this nightmare hole. I tried to read it but it was breaking the flow a lot so I went back to the main article. Best to keep things self-contained, or at least not make the plot (apparently) hinge on something that happens in another SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus christ, kaktus.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that was extremely long, but so worth the pay-off! The logs were so fun to read through, and kind of remind me of the logs of SCP-093 and SCP-2935. I also like how it leaves you hanging with a few questions, which I think is a great indicator of a well-crafted story. It's a +1 from me!

Edit: I just realized that you also wrote SCP-2935, which is hands-down my favourite SCP on this site. You're the best man! Keep up the awesome articles!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty good. Despite it's size I read through to the end, along with the crosslinks, and as per my standard, +1.
However, I've been going through skip after skip and as opposed to being a contained anomaly, and upvoted or downvoted based on that, they're all narratives, and are getting my upvote based on their narrative hook.
I keep being told that the current standard (2019) of authorship is superior to series i, I'm doubting that though. The authorship here is excellent, but it's of a different kind. It's comparing apples or oranges. The SCP takes a backseat to everything that happens to, in, or because of it.
Watch me get flamed but in my head, SCP is about containing creepypastas, whereas this is itself a creepypasta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized, after first having read this a long time ago, that I had committed the egregious crime of not having upvoted it.
It was sitting at +1400 (repeat: +1400), and apparently received another while I was reading, as it is now at +1402.

In other news, congrats, Kaktus, on an incredible SCP with an astronomical rating.

item.title: Re: ~Final Log~
item.content:encoded: So that was Pesterbot! Thought it might be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like a lot of the anomalies in this are modified (?) versions of other SCPs. Could we get a list of the SCPs featured, and possibly how they're different?

For example, Dr. Bright's amulet makes him live forever, rather than copying his consciousness (unless that was meant to be a misunderstanding on Dr. Scott's part).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so basically:

Site-13 was kicked from an alternate reality of the foundation using a machine called “The Thresher” as a last resort.

The reason they had to do this was because the machine they used to kill SCPs after testing was broken and created the leech creatures after a young boy who could suck blood from people’s skin was incinerated.

The black goop is implied to be the leftovers from incinerated anomalies.

The humanoid that writes on the walls is what causes the “fire ear” that causes loud noises to trigger explosions from people inflicted with it, and is an anomaly that wasn’t incinerated, just like the faceless humanoids.

Those inflicted with “fire ear” are part of a group of survivors kicked out due to their predicament.

The reason Site-13 appears to change its hallways is because of the activation of The Thresher.

Not confusing at all.

+1

item.title: Outstanding work
item.content:encoded: I found this via a 001 entry. I’m absolutely sure I don’t get half the references in here. But, this is one of the best skips I’ve read, period. Bravissimo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good god, recent events have given a whole new spin on this, let me tell you.

Of course two realities get fucked over by crazy multi-godzilla SCP insanity because of Paul Flipping Manafort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, shit. Feels like I just read through an entire canon again!

Overall, huge fan of the concept — fucked up timelines are always cool, and this one does it particularly well. I'm also a sucker for exploration logs, so massive bonus points for making me read a whole pile of 'em, surprisingly they manage not to drag on at all.

Buuuut, I have to agree with earlier criticsm that the Samsara logs just don't fit in with the rest, and I also agree that they might be better off as some sort of tale.

All the rest of the page absolutely carries them, though, so it's still a +1 from me either way.

item.title: Re: What happened to Site-13?
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint is that the addendums containing exploration/recovery logs read less like transcripts and more like scripts. Like for a play. There's description where there doesn't need to be (it is not usually relevant to the mission or the SCP when a task force member laughs), and insufficient description in other places (those Samsara guys sure do pause a lot, instead of, say, take a moment to survey their surroundings, or something similarly more detailed).

Continuing with that second point, there are so, SO many spots where you repeat verbs way too many times in quick succession. Pauses, motions, etc.

You also tend to stray away from the "toneless/impartial" inflection I would expect from an in-universe SCP employee typing up an actual transcript. I don't really know how to describe it better than that. I'm talking about your narrative scene/event descriptions, not your characters' dialogue. It causes the addendums to lose a certain sense of professionalism.

With all that in mind, I did like this enough to re-read it. It's good overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an impressive SCP. There are tons of moving parts here, and I don't think I fully get 100% of what's going on. I wish I didn't postpone reading this for so long because it's absolutely amazing. Probably one of my top 10 SCPs on the site. Despite its absolutely crazy length, this manages to be engaging throughout. Great stuff.

item.title: Exploration Logs
item.content:encoded: Damn, thanks to this I have a newfound love for Exploration Logs.

item.title: SCP-3000?
item.content:encoded: I look at every post in this discussion and didn’t see SCP-3000 referenced. Is the massive dead sea creature mentioned in Debrief Interview 6 supposed to be SCP-3000?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression the tongue-woman was Vera Hadley, as she said she was going to go down to be with Elijah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, THIS is a goddamn good SCP.
I don't know how this felt under my radar, but since I am more recent reader of everything in the wiki rather than just the classics (which I read like back in 2013 or something like that) I don't feel at fault.

Still, extremely cool. I love how the story doesn't end just in an unclear way. It is a very good ending.

Even if I wonder what does it mean to contain God. I mean, what. But that's another matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hol up. So there is this guy that only needs to feed blood to survive, and the Foundation actively tries to kill him…
Duke?

item.title: Re: Translation to german
item.content:encoded: Go for it my guy.

item.title: Re: Translation to german
item.content:encoded:
WAIT… You want to translate this behemoth?
Have fun, I say.
